coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional carbon dot/MXene heterojunctions for alleviation of tumor hypoxia and enhanced sonodynamic therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Geng, Bijiang
Xu, Shuang
Shen, Longxiang
Fang, Fuling
Shi, Wenyan
Pan, Dengyu
coredata.dc:description: TiO2-based semiconductor nanosonosensitizers have been extensively explored for sonodynamic therapy (SDT) against deep-seated tumors, but their clinical applications are limited by low reactive oxygen species (ROS) quantum yields owing to their rather wide bandgaps (∼3.2 eV). Herein, we report Ti3C2-based two-dimensional (2D) MXene nanosheets with a bandgap of only ∼0.94 eV, induced by surface terminations (-F, –OH), were employed as a novel narrow-bandgap nanosonosensitizer for effective SDT. To improve the stability and SDT performance of the MXene nanosheets, 0D/2D hybrid heterojunctions (HJs) were also introduced between Ti3C2 nanosheets and graphitic N doped carbon dots. Taking the advantage of the HJ-enhanced carrier transfers, the designed CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs exhibited a remarkably enhanced ROS generation efficiency, which was 3.07 times than that of commercial TiO2 nanoparticles. The CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs were also found to offer enhanced photothermal conversion efficiency in the NIR-II biowindow (64.5%). By using the mild photothermal effect to alleviate tumor hypoxia in the “cold” tumor microenvironment (TME), complete tumor ablation was realized by the mild NIR-II PTT-enhanced deep-tissue SDT. This work presents the promising potential of MXene-based 2D materials for noninvasive SDT of deep-seated tumors.

head.ce:title: Multifunctional carbon dot/MXene heterojunctions for alleviation of tumor hypoxia and enhanced sonodynamic therapy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: TiO2-based semiconductor nanosonosensitizers have been extensively explored for sonodynamic therapy (SDT) against deep-seated tumors, but their clinical applications are limited by low reactive oxygen species (ROS) quantum yields owing to their rather wide bandgaps (∼3.2 eV). Herein, we report Ti3C2-based two-dimensional (2D) MXene nanosheets with a bandgap of only ∼0.94 eV, induced by surface terminations (-F, –OH), were employed as a novel narrow-bandgap nanosonosensitizer for effective SDT. To improve the stability and SDT performance of the MXene nanosheets, 0D/2D hybrid heterojunctions (HJs) were also introduced between Ti3C2 nanosheets and graphitic N doped carbon dots. Taking the advantage of the HJ-enhanced carrier transfers, the designed CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs exhibited a remarkably enhanced ROS generation efficiency, which was 3.07 times than that of commercial TiO2 nanoparticles. The CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs were also found to offer enhanced photothermal conversion efficiency in the NIR-II biowindow (64.5%). By using the mild photothermal effect to alleviate tumor hypoxia in the “cold” tumor microenvironment (TME), complete tumor ablation was realized by the mild NIR-II PTT-enhanced deep-tissue SDT. This work presents the promising potential of MXene-based 2D materials for noninvasive SDT of deep-seated tumors.

Overall scheme to show the main synthesis procedures of CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs and their applications for mild PTT-enhanced SDT.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: To address the continuing threat of cancer, versatile innovative therapeutic strategies have been considered as promising alternatives to conventional surgery, chemotherapy, and radiotherapy [1–4]. Owing to the considerable merits of large penetration depth (>10 cm), minimal invasiveness, and limited side effects, sonodynamic therapy (SDT) in the presence of effective sonosensitizers, activated by low-intensity ultrasound (US) wave, has been extensively explored to treat deep-seated tumors [5–7]. Sonosensitizers are activated by ultrasound triggered cavitation to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) with nearby oxygen molecules, and subsequently induce cell apoptosis and necrosis [8]. Considering the SDT mechanism, the co-existence of effective sonosensitizers and rich oxygen molecules in the tumor microenvironment (TME) is critical but challenging for the success of SDT.

Currently, many organic small molecules have been employed as sonosensitizers, including porphyrin derivatives, phthalocyanine, erythrosine, and rose bengal [9–13], but their poor chemical stability, potential phototoxicity, and limited tumor accumulation substantially restricted their SDT applications [10,14–17]. Semiconducting inorganic nanomaterials are also being extensively explored for SDT. Of them, TiO2 nanoparticles or nanosheets are most explored because of reduced phototoxicity and unique energy-band structures [18,19]. Unfortunately, the shortcomings of TiO2-based nanomaterials including low aqueous dispersibility and poor biodegradability severely hinder their biomedical applications [20,21]. Particularly, the fast electron-hole pair recombination rate and the wide bandgap of TiO2 (3.2 eV) lead to a low ROS quantum yield under US stimulation [22]. To enhance the SDT performance, great efforts have been made to reduce their bandgaps by introducing oxygen vacancy in TiO2 (TiO1+x, bandgap 2.05 eV) [6] or assembling TiO2 with noble metals to form heterojunction structures (Au–TiO2, 2.9 eV) [20]. Other Ti-based semiconductors such as titanium hydride (TiH1.924) nanodots [5] with a lower bandgap (2.7 eV) were also explored as an enhanced sonosensitizers for SDT. Although these strategies have been conducted to improve the ROS generation efficiency of Ti-based nanosonosensitizers, there is still much room to develop high-efficiency sonosensitizers with narrow bandgaps to overcome carrier recombination and to further improve the its ROS generation capability.

As emerging 2D nanomaterials, MXenes with large surface area, high electronic conductivity, high catalytic activity, and other attractive properties, have been extensively explored for various applications including catalysis [23,24], energy storage [25–27], chemical sensing [28–30], wireless communication [31], and electromagnetic interference shielding [32,33]. Recently, the biomedical applications of MXene-based nanomaterials, such as biosensing, antibacterial, photoacoustic imaging and photothermal therapy (PTT) [34–37], have also been explored owing to their excellent biocompatibility, biodegradability, and high photothermal conversion efficiencies. Among discovered MXenes, Ti3C2 MXenes have aroused the most attention owing to easy fabrication, low cost, and better performances. Recently, commonly metallic Ti3C2 nanosheets (NSs) were found to be converted into narrow bandgap semiconductors owing to surface terminations (-F, =O, –OH) [24,38]. Qin et al. reported Ti3C2Tx nanosheets (T = F and OH) with a narrow bandgaps (∼0.75 V) for photocatalytic applications [39], suggesting their promise in the SDT application.

Ti3AlC2 was obtained from Forsman Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). ICG and pyrene (purity > 98%) were purchased from TCI. BPEI (Mn ∼ 70, 000 Da) was obtained from Aladdin. DPBF, TEMP, and MTT were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. HF aqueous solution (40%) and HNO3 were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd. TPAOH were obtained from J&K Scientific Co., Ltd. DMEM, RPMI 1640, FBS, and trypsin were purchased from Gibco.

Initially, multi-layer Ti3C2Tx NSs were prepared from Ti3AlC2 bulk ceramic by etching Al layer. Typically, Ti3AlC2 (2 g) was dispersed in HF aqueous solution (40%, 50 mL) and stirred for 72 h to obtain multi-layer Ti3C2Tx NSs. After centrifugation at 12, 000 rpm, the obtained multi-layer Ti3C2Tx NSs were dispersed in a 100 mL TPAOH solution and stirred for another 72 h. Then, the ultrathin Ti3C2Tx NSs were obtained after centrifugation at 12, 000 rpm.

Pyrene (2 g) was initially utilized to synthesize TNP through the nitration reaction in HNO3 (160 mL). We then synthesized the NIR–II–responsive CDs through an ultrafast (5 min) microwave reaction using TNP and BPEI as the precursor. Typically, 0.05 g TNP and 0.4 g BPEI were dispersed in 10 mL DI water. After the ultrafast microwave reaction at 200 °C for 5 min, the obtained CDs were dialyzed for 48 h and dried for following characterization.

For the synthesis of CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs, the CDs (0.4 mg/mL) were assembled on the surface of Ti3C2Tx NSs (0.1 mg/mL) by vigorous stirring for 12 h. After stirring, the un-assembled CDs were removed through centrifugation. By determining the changed mass of Ti3C2Tx NSs before and after CD assembling, the loading ratio of CDs can be calculated to be 400%.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of the Ti3C2Tx NSs, CDs, and CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs were acquired on JEM-2100F. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images of HF-etched Ti3C2Tx NSs were recorded on a field-emission S-4800 microscope. Atomic force microscope (AFM) images of Ti3C2Tx NSs and CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs were acquired on a Nano Man VS system. XPS and FT-IR were performed using Kratos Axis Ultra DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer and Nicolet AVATAR 370 FT-IR, respectively. UV–vis–NIR absorption and fluorescence spectra of these samples were detected by Agilent Cary 5000 and Hitachi 7000, respectively. ESR spectra of Ti3C2Tx NSs and CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs were recorded on a Bruker EMXplus ESR spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative analysis of the generation of 1O2
ce:section.ce:para: For 1O2 detection, 20 μg DPBF was added to the CD@Ti3C2Tx HJ solution (0.5 mg/mL, 1 mL). The absorbance at 418 nm of the mixture solution was detected to quantify the oxidation rate of DPBF by CD@Ti3C2Tx under US irradiation (50 kHz, 3.0 W/cm2). For comparison, Ti3C2Tx NSs, CDs and commercial TiO2 nanoparticles at the same concentration (0.5 mg/mL) were also measured under the same US irradiation conditions.

For ESR measurement of 1O2, 20 μL TEMP was added to the CD@Ti3C2Tx HJ solution (0.5 mg/mL, 1 mL). ESR spectrometer was utilized to detect 1O2 generation by CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs under US irradiation (50 kHz, 3.0 W/cm2) for 2 min.

For the photothermal performance measurements, a centrifuge tube containing CD@Ti3C2Tx, CDs, or Ti3C2Tx at the same concentration (0.5 mg/mL) was exposed to 1064 nm laser (0.6 W/cm2, 5 min). The infrared thermal camera (Fluke, USA) was utilized to measure the temperature change of centrifuge tubes every 30 s. The photothermal stability and photothermal conversion efficiency of CD@Ti3C2Tx, CDs, or Ti3C2Tx were measured according to the previous reports [42].

We utilized a three-electrode system to measure the cyclic voltammetry (CV) of CDs using electrochemical workstation (CHI 660D, China). The CD-deposited platinum sheet, Ag/AgCl, and TBAPF6 were used as working electrode, reference electrode, and electrolyte, respectively. The energy level of CB was calculated to be −0.83 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) at pH 7.0. According to the equation: ENHE = EAg/AgCl + 0.059 × pH - E0 Ag/AgCl, ECB of CDs versus the NHE can be calculated to be −0.61 V.

4T1 cells, MG-63 cells, and human mesenchymal stem cells (hMSCs) were purchased from ATCC and cultured in DMEM or RPMI 1640. For the cytotoxicity test, 4T1, MG-63, or hMSCs were seeded and incubated with CD@Ti3C2Tx, CDs, or Ti3C2Tx, respectively. MTT assay was performed according to the previous reports [14]. For the mild PTT-enhanced SDT, after 4T1 cells incubated with CD@Ti3C2Tx for 4 h, US (50 kHz, 3.0 W/cm2) or 1064 nm laser irradiation (T < 42 °C) was conducted for 5 min, followed by another 24 h incubation. For live/dead staining, calcein AM and PI were utilized to stain the 4T1 cells under different treatments (including control, NIR-II, US, CD@Ti3C2Tx, Ti3C2Tx + US, CD@Ti3C2Tx + NIR-II, CD@Ti3C2Tx + US, CD@Ti3C2Tx + NIR-II + US). The images were acquired by an Olympus microscope.

5-week-old female Balb/c nude mice were purchased from SLAC (China), and all animal experiments were performed under the permission by Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of Shanghai University (SYXK 2019-0020). ICG-labeled CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs (5.0 mg/kg) were intravenously injected to the mice. The in vivo NIR fluorescence imaging was performed at varied time points. For the synthesis of ICG-labeled CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs, ICG (20 μg/mL) was assembled on the surface of CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs by vigorous stirring in PBS for 24 h. After stirring, the un-assembled ICG was removed through centrifugation.

For immunohistochemistry analysis, CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs (5.0 mg/kg) were intravenously injected to the mice. The NIR-II laser was employed to 4T1 tumors at 24 h post-injection with the temperature in tumor tissue maintained at ∼45 °C. After irradiation, these tumors were immediately excised for hypoxia staining assay. Notably, pimonidazole hydrochloride was injected into the mice via intraperitoneal injection before the irradiation for 80 min. In the hypoxic TME, pimonidazole hydrochloride would transfer to stable adducts, which binds to the FITC-Mab1, allowing the detection by immunohistochemistry analysis. Alex 488-conjugated goat anti-mouse secondary antibody (Jackon Inc.) and DAPI (Invitrogen) were also utilized for hypoxia staining and cell nucleus staining, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo mild PTT enhanced SDT
ce:section.ce:para: Eight groups (n = 5) were divided as follow: Group 1: control, Group 2: NIR-II (T < 45 °C), Group 3: US (50 kHz, 3.0 W/cm2, 5 min), Group 4: CD@Ti3C2Tx (5 mg/kg), Group 5: Ti3C2Tx + US, Group 6: CD@Ti3C2Tx + NIR-II (T < 45 °C), Group 7: CD@Ti3C2Tx + US, Group 8: CD@Ti3C2Tx + NIR-II (T < 45 °C) + US. Body weight and tumor sizes were measured every days and every two days, respectively. Tumors and major organs were collected on the second day post treatment and at the end of the treatment for in vivo H&E staining and histopathological measurements, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs
ce:section.ce:para: The main synthesis procedures of CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs were composed of three main steps, as depicted in Fig. 1 a. Firstly, multi-layer Ti3C2 NSs with terminal –F were synthesized from the MAX phase Ti3AlC2 ceramics through a modified liquid-phase exfoliation method using 40% hydrofluoric acid (HF) as the etching agent to remove the Al layer. Secondly, the resulting multi-layer Ti3C2Tx NSs were further delaminated into negatively charged Ti3C2 NSs with terminal –F and –OH through TPAOH intercalation. Finally, the positively charged NIR–II–responsive CDs were easily deposited on the negatively charged Ti3C2Tx NSs to obtain the CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs via a strong electrostatic interaction. Before deposition, the graphitic N doped CDs with strong NIR-II absorbance were synthesized through a microwave reaction using 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (TNP) and branched polyethylenimine (BPEI, Mñ70000) as the precursors. The loading efficiency of CDs onto Ti3C2Tx NSs was calculated to be 400% owing to the strong surface charge driven assembly. By virtue of the hydrophilic CD loading, the as-prepared CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs exhibited remarkably improved stability in H2O, PBS, and DMEM, without showing obvious agglomeration after storing for different periods of time (0, 1, and 7 D) (Fig. S1).

The microstructure of Ti3C2Tx NSs, CDs, and CD-loaded Ti3C2Tx HJs was investigated by SEM, TEM, and AFM. After HF etching from bulk Ti3AlC2 ceramics, the as-synthesized Ti3C2Tx were still in the form of multi-layer NSs (Fig. 1b), which suggested that they were unsuitable for the following intravenous administration and accumulation into tumor tissue. The nanoscale Ti3C2Tx NSs with a uniform dispersion were synthesized form multi-layer Ti3C2Tx NSs through further TPAOH intercalating and exfoliating (Fig. 1c). For NIR–II–responsive CDs, the TEM image presented in Fig. S2a revealed that the average diameter of monodisperse CDs was 4.3 ± 0.7 nm. Meanwhile, the high crystallinity of well-dispersed CDs with a 0.21 nm crystal spacing was observed in the HRTEM image (Fig. S2b). After depositing 0D CDs on 2D Ti3C2Tx NSs, DLS measurements revealed that the average diameter of CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs was 91.9 nm (Fig. S3a), which was comparable to that of pristine Ti3C2Tx NSs (85.2 nm). TEM and HRTEM images of HJs revealed that the monodisperse CDs were well assembled on the surface of Ti3C2Tx NSs without obvious aggregation (Fig. 1d and e), confirming the successful assembling of CDs. The successful loading of CDs on the Ti3C2Tx NSs was further demonstrated by AFM image (Fig. S4), which revealed that the thickness of Ti3C2Tx NSs increased from 2 nm to 4 nm after CD loading. Zeta potential measurements were then performed to confirm the strong electrostatic interaction between CDs and Ti3C2Tx NSs. As shown in Fig. S3b, compared with negatively charged Ti3C2Tx NSs (−27.9 ± 2.5 mV), the zeta potential of CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs was reversed to 4.8 ± 0.8 mV owing to the loading of positively charged CDs (20.8 ± 1.6 mV).

To further confirm that the NIR–II–responsive CDs were successfully deposited on Ti3C2Tx NSs, we performed XRD, XPS, and FT-IR to unveil the chemical composition of Ti3C2Tx NSs, CDs, and CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs. Fig. 1f exhibited the XRD pattern of un-etched Ti3AlC2, which was consistent with the MAX phase Ti3AlC2 (JCPDS card no. 52–0875) [43]. In the XRD pattern of post-etched Ti3C2Tx NSs, the disappearance of the strongest diffraction peak at 2θ ≈ 39° illustrated the elimination of Al layer [44], verifying the transformation from bulk Ti3AlC2 ceramics to Ti3C2Tx NSs. Apart from the diffraction peak of MAX phase Ti3C2, a broad characteristic peak at 2θ ≈ 26° corresponded to the (002) layer spacing of CDs [45] appeared in the XRD pattern of CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs (Fig. 1g). The highly N-doped characteristic of CDs (15.6 at.%) can be observed in the XPS spectra (Fig. S5), illustrating the CDs contains pyrrolic N and graphitic N. After CD loading, the strong signal of N 1s at around 399 eV can be detected in the survey XPS spectrum of CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs compared with that of pristine Ti3C2Tx NSs (Fig. 1h), indicating the successful deposition of CDs into the surface of Ti3C2Tx NSs. Furthermore, the strong signal of –F and –OH was observed in the XPS spectra of these samples (Fig. 1h, j), confirming that the successful preparation of F and OH-terminated Ti3C2Tx NSs. The chemical status of Ti was detailed investigated by the high-resolution Ti 2p spectrum of Ti3C2Tx NSs (Fig. 1i) and CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs (Fig. 1j). Two peaks at 454.7 eV and 460.6 eV illustrated the presence of Ti2+ and Ti4+ in the pristine Ti3C2Tx NSs. Notably, a new binding energy peak at 458.0 eV suggested the presence of Ti3+ in the CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs [6], which could be ascribed to partial oxidation of Ti3C2Tx NSs during deposition. Apart from the change of Ti 2p spectrum, the successful loading of CDs was further verified by the high-resolution N 1s spectrum of CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs (Fig. 1j). Two peaks of CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs at 400.5 eV and 398.9 eV were assigned to graphitic N and pyrrolic N [46,47], respectively. In addition, the existence of Ti–C, O–H, Ti–O, C–O, CC, and C–N in CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs was verified by the high-resolution O 1s, C 1s, and FTIR spectra (Fig. S3c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced sonodynamic and NIR-II photothermal performance of CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs
ce:section.ce:para: Having demonstrated the successful fabrication of CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs, we then assessed the enhanced sonodynamic performance of CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs by measuring their 1O2 generation efficiency under US irradiation using DPBF as a ROS probe (Fig. 2 a). After adding the DPBF probe to pristine Ti3C2Tx NS or CD@Ti3C2Tx HJ solution, we utilized the UV–vis absorption spectrum to monitor the change of DPBF characteristic absorbance peak (418 nm) in the presence of US irradiation. As shown in Fig. 2b and c, the 418 nm absorbance of DPBF in the Ti3C2Tx NS or CD@Ti3C2Tx HJ solution exhibited a significant decrease within 5 min as the US irradiation time increased, suggesting the high-efficiency ROS generation capability. In contrast, no obvious oxidation of DPBF by CD@Ti3C2Tx alone or US alone can be detected (Fig. S7), clearly illustrating the US-activated ROS generation behavior of CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs. Importantly, the rate constant of DPBF degradation in the CD@Ti3C2Tx HJ group was detected to be 0.166 min−1 (Fig. 2d), which was 2.37 times than that of pristine Ti3C2Tx NS group (0.070 min−1) under the same US irradiation condition, indicating that the sonodynamic performance of Ti3C2Tx NSs was significantly enhanced after CD loading. We also performed the electron spin resonance (ESR) to compare the 1O2 generation between Ti3C2Tx and CD@Ti3C2Tx sonosensitizers (Fig, 2e). The characteristic peak of 1O2 in the CD@Ti3C2Tx plus US group displayed a significant increase than that of pristine Ti3C2Tx plus US group, further confirming the improvement of the ROS generation efficiency of CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs. Subsequently, we compared the sonodynamic performance of CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs with the commercial TiO2 nanoparticles, which were the most representative paradigm of inorganic sonosensitizers [5]. Fig. S8 revealed that the rate constant of Ti3C2Tx NSs (0.166 min−1) was 3.07 times than that of commercial TiO2 nanoparticles (0.054 min−1), demonstrating the superior 1O2 generation ability of HJs. These results verified that the fabrication of 0D/2D HJ structure by assembling CDs on the surface of Ti3C2Tx NSs remarkably enhanced the sonodynamic performance, which could be ascribed to the enhanced carrier transfer and reduced electron-hole recombination.

Considering the enhanced sonodynamic performance of CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs, we then established a model of the energy band structure of the HJs to unveil the probable mechanism of improved sonodynamic effect. The absorption spectrum was first measured to calculate the optical bandgap of CDs and Ti3C2Tx NSs. According to the Tauc plot (Fig. S10a) and the absorption spectrum (Fig. 2h), the bandgap energy (Eg) of CDs was determined to be 2.2 eV. The CV curve of CDs was then measured to detect the conduction band (CB) bottom potential, which was calculated to be −0.83 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) at pH 7.0 (Fig. S10b). Based on the equation in the Experiment section, the CB potential (ECB) of CDs versus the normal hydrogen electrode (NHS) can be determined to be −0.63 V (vs. NHS). For Ti3C2Tx NSs, the VB potential were determined to be 0.45 V according to the XPS-VB spectrum (Fig. S10d). The narrow bandgap characteristic of Ti3C2Tx NSs was confirmed by the Tauc plot (Fig. S10c), indicating that the bandgap of Ti3C2Tx NSs was only 0.94 eV. Correspondingly, the CB bottom of CDs (−0.63 V) located above that of Ti3C2Tx NSs (−0.49 V), which gave an energy difference to drive the excited electrons rapidly migrating from CDs to Ti3C2Tx NSs (Fig. 2g). Notably, compared with pristine CDs, the significant PL quenching was observed in the fluorescence spectrum of CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs (Fig. 2f), further demonstrating that the excited electrons transferred from the CB of CDs to that of Ti3C2Tx NSs instead of recombining with the hole in the valence band (VB) of CDs. US-activated electrons transferred to Ti3C2Tx NSs will further react with oxygen (O2) to produce a large amount of 1O2. Taking the advantage of the outstanding conductivity of Ti3C2Tx NSs, the formed electron sink by HJs on the CD@Ti3C2Tx interface effectively enhanced the electron transfer and prevented the electron-hole recombination, which significantly improved the US-activated ROS generation capability of Ti3C2Tx NSs.

We then compared the photothermal performance of CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs with pristine Ti3C2Tx NSs to further verify the mechanism of HJ-enhanced sonodynamic and photothermal effect. The enhanced optical properties of the as-synthesized CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs were first evaluated by comparing with those of pristine Ti3C2Tx NSs and CDs using UV–vis–NIR absorption spectra. The both black solutions of CD and Ti3C2Tx NS exhibited a strong absorption in the NIR region (Fig. 2h), even in the suitable NIR-II biowindow with strong tissue-penetrating capability [42,48,49]. Moreover, the remarkably enhanced absorption can be observed in the CD@Ti3C2Tx HJ solution (0.25 mg/mL), which contains CDs (0.2 mg/mL) and Ti3C2Tx (0.05 mg/mL), suggesting that the enhanced absorption could be ascribed to the changed photo-physical characteristics of HJs rather than the simple physical mixture effect of CDs and Ti3C2Tx NSs. We then evaluated the enhanced photothermal effect of CD@Ti3C2Tx by comparing their temperature variation curves under NIR-II laser (0.6 W/cm2) with that of pristine CDs and Ti3C2Tx NSs. Fig. 2i revealed that the temperature of CD@Ti3C2Tx HJ solution increased to 55.6 °C, while that of CDs and Ti3C2Tx NSs only increased to 49.0 and 46.3 °C, respectively, clearly verifying the synergistically enhanced NIR-II photothermal effect of HJs. Furthermore, the visual temperature variation of CDs, Ti3C2Tx NSs, and CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs can be observed in Fig. S11. The remarkable laser-power-dependent (Fig. S12b) and concentration-dependent photothermal effects (Fig. S12a) in the NIR-II window of CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs were further investigated at different power density (0.2–0.8 W/cm2) and CD@Ti3C2Tx concentration (0.1–0.4 mg/mL), respectively, confirming that the mild PTT can be easily realized at the safe power density (0.6 W/cm2). Apart from the enhanced NIR-II absorption and photothermal heating effect, the enhanced photothermal stability of HJs was demonstrated by periodically exposing CDs, Ti3C2Tx NSs, and CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs to 1064 nm laser for ten on/off cycles. Significantly, the photothermal heating effect of CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs exhibited no obvious deterioration compared with that of pristine Ti3C2Tx NSs (Fig. 2k), confirming the enhanced photothermal stability of HJs even in the continuous NIR-II laser irradiation. It is well known that the photothermal conversion efficiency (η) of a potential PTT agent is the main parameter to assess its photothermal performance [42,50]. After monitoring the photothermal heating and cooling curves (Fig. 2j and Fig. S12c), the η 1064 of CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs was calculated to be 64.5%, which was significantly higher than that of CDs (49.6%) and Ti3C2Tx NSs (40.1%) at the same concentration (0.5 mg/mL), demonstrating the enhanced photothermal properties of Ti3C2Tx NSs after the deposition of NIR–II–responsive CDs. Especially, the η
1064 of CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs (64.5%) was also remarkably higher than that of previous reported PTT agents, such as noble metals (21%) [51], metal oxides (39.4%) [52,53], polymers (43.4%) [48], and organic molecules (41.4%) [54]. Similar to the mechanism of the enhanced sonodynamic performance of CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs, NIR-II laser-activated electrons would transfer from the CB of CDs to that of Ti3C2Tx NSs. Subsequently, abundant free carriers accumulated in the surface of Ti3C2Tx NSs could induce the localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) effect [55,56], which significantly enhanced the NIR-II absorption and photothermal effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro mild NIR-II PTT-enhanced SDT
ce:section.ce:para: Recently, deep-tissue PTT triggered by the 1064 nm laser is also considered as a noninvasive tumor therapeutic modality with desirable penetration depth and high selectivity [57,58]. Generally, rigorous PTT-induced hyperthermia over 50 °C is required to achieve completely tumor elimination, while such a high temperature will also cause obvious damage to normal tissues [59]. In this situation, we assumed that mild photothermal effects (∼45 °C) were employed as an adjuvant therapy rather than directly ablating tumor tissues. More importantly, mild PTT has been demonstrated to improve oxygen supply by enhancing intratumor blood flow and permeability [40,41]. Therefore, rational combination of SDT with mild PTT in the NIR-II region offers a promising strategy to overcome the shortcomings of both SDT and PTT, which may alleviate hypoxia in TME and achieve highly efficient synergistic anticancer therapy (Fig. 3 a).

We first evaluated the in vitro mild NIR-II PTT-enhanced sonodynamic cancer therapy induced by CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs. MTT assays were initially conducted to assess the biocompatibility of the potential sonosensitizers and PTT agents. No significant cytotoxicity of as-prepared CDs, Ti3C2Tx NSs, and CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs against cancer cells (4T1 and MG-63) and human mesenchymal stem cells (hMSCs) was observed (Fig. 3b and Figs. S13–14). Subsequently, the viability of 4T1 cells treated with CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs plus periodical NIR-II laser irradiation was detected to evaluate the in vitro mild PTT efficiency. Owing to a slight increase of the cell culture temperature (42 °C) under mild PTT, the viability of CD@Ti3C2Tx-treated 4T1 cells only decreased to 75.4% (Fig. 3c). It should be noted that the viability of CD@Ti3C2Tx-treated 4T1 cells under continuous 1064 nm laser treatment was only 3.6% when the temperature of cell culture increased above 50 °C (Fig. S15). Specifically, when US (50 kHz, 3.0 W/cm2, 5 min) and 1064 nm laser (0.6 W/cm2, 5 min) irradiation were performed simultaneously, the cell viability dramatically decreased to 4.7%, which was much less than that of CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs plus US irradiation only (19.5%), clearly demonstrating the synergetic anticancer effect of mild PTT-enhanced sonodynamic cancer therapy. Furthermore, we also carried out the live/dead co-staining assays to confirm the excellent anticancer effect of synergetic PTT and SDT. The strong PI signal with red fluorescence was observed in the CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs + NIR-II + US group (Fig. 3d), suggesting that nearly all of the 4T1 cells treated with CD@Ti3C2Tx were dead. These results forcefully demonstrated that the mild NIR-II PTT would significant improve the SDT efficiency of CD@Ti3C2Tx owing to the improved membrane permeability and cell uptake of CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs by virtue of the NIR-II laser irradiation.

We next performed the DCFH-DA staining assay to directly determine the intracellular ROS levels and investigate the mechanism of CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs as a sonosensitizer under US irradiation to efficient kill cancer cells. No significant ROS fluorescence signal of 4T1 cells were detected in the control group, NIR-II group, US group, CD@Ti3C2Tx group, CD + US group, Ti3C2Tx + US group, and CD@Ti3C2Tx + NIR-II group (Fig. 3e). In contrast, the 4T1 cells incubated with CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs under US irradiation exhibited obvious green fluorescence, illustrating US-activated intracellular ROS generation. Significantly, the strongest fluorescence was detected in the 4T1 cells incubated with CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs plus US and 1064 nm laser irradiation, confirming that the mild PTT effect could induce the alleviation of tumor hypoxia and replenished O2 for enhanced ROS generation under US stimulation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo mild PTT-alleviated tumor hypoxia
ce:section.ce:para: Prior to evaluate the mild PTT-enhanced sonodynamic therapy in vivo, we used NIR fluorescent imaging to verify the tumor targeting ability of CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs. Additionally, NIR fluorescent imaging-guided cancer therapy would help us to determine the optimal US and 1064 nm laser irradiation time points, achieving more effective and accurate mild PTT-enhanced SDT. In this situation, we initially utilized indocyanine green (ICG) to label CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs. The obvious fluorescent signals in the tumor tissue were detected after intravenously injecting ICG-labeled CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs for 8 h (Fig. 4 a), confirming the excellent tumor targeting ability of CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs owing to the widely reported EPR effect. Especially, the strongest fluorescent signals of ICG-labeled CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs at the tumor sites can be observed at 24 h post-injection (Fig. 4b, d), which guided us the optimal time for US and 1064 nm laser irradiation. Apart from in vivo NIR fluorescent imaging, we further performed the in vivo infrared thermal imaging to verify the accumulation of CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs in the tumor tissue at post-injection of 24 h. The temperature of the tumor tissue in the CD@Ti3C2Tx group rapidly increased to 55.0 °C under NIR-II laser, which is significantly higher than that of the tumor tissue in the control group (38.8 °C) (Fig. 4c and Fig. S16). The infrared thermal imaging results not only further demonstrated the tumor targeting ability of CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs, but also indicated the promising application of CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs in efficient NIR-II PTT.

Hypoxia in solid tumors induced by the abnormalities of tumor vessels often renders the failure of most standard cancer therapies including chemotherapy, radiotherapy, PDT, chemodynamic therapy (CDT), and SDT [60–62]. Therefore, the alleviation of hypoxia in TME is of great importance for SDT efficiency. In this situation, we conducted the immunofluorescence hypoxia staining assay to verify the alleviation of hypoxia in the tumor tissue induced by mild NIR-II PTT. As expected, the hypoxia signal of tumor slices in the CD@Ti3C2Tx plus NIR-II laser group was less than that of the control group, only NIR-II laser group, and only CD@Ti3C2Tx group (Fig. 4e), illustrating that the severe tumor hypoxia was alleviated by the mild NIR-II PTT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo mild NIR-II PTT-enhanced SDT
ce:section.ce:para: Having verified the tumor targeting ability of CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs and the alleviation of hypoxia in TME by mild PTT, we next evaluated the mild NIR-II PTT-enhanced sonodynamic cancer therapy using CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs. 40 4T1tumor-bearing mice were randomly divided into eight groups (n = 5): (1) Control; (2) NIR-II (0.6 W/cm2, 5 min, T < 45 °C); (3) US (50 kHz, 3.0 W/cm2); (4) CD@Ti3C2Tx; (5) Ti3C2Tx + US; (6) CD@Ti3C2Tx + NIR-II; (7) CD@Ti3C2Tx + US; (8) CD@Ti3C2Tx + NIR-II + US. The 1064 nm laser and US irradiation were carried out after intravenously injecting of CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs for 24 h (Fig. 5 a). The tumor growth and mice weight were monitored during various treatments to assess the therapeutic effect of mild NIR-II PTT-enhanced SDT. No obvious tumor growth inhibition was observed in the control group, only NIR-II group, only US group, and only CD@Ti3C2Tx group (Fig. 5b). In addition, the tumor growth in the CD@Ti3C2Tx plus mild PTT group exhibited a slight inhibition (35%) since the temperature in the tumor site remains below 45 °C under a 1064 nm laser irradiation (0.6 W/cm2). For US-activated SDT, the inhibition ratio of tumor growth in the CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs plus US irradiation group was calculated to be 81%, which was much higher than that of Ti3C2Tx NSs plus US irradiation group (37%), demonstrating the enhanced SDT performance of CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs. Significantly, the tumors in the CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs plus US and 1064 nm laser irradiation group were completely eradiated (Fig. 5e and Fig. S17), indicating that the SDT performance of CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs would be enhanced by virtue of mild PTT-alleviated tumor hypoxia. Moreover, no obvious pulmonary metastatic nodules can be observed in the combined PTT and SDT group (Fig. 5d), further confirming the synergistic enhanced photothermal-sonodynamic cancer therapy. We also performed the H&E staining of tumors in each group after different treatments for 24 h. Fig. 5f revealed that the 4T1 tumor cells were completely damaged in the CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs plus US and 1064 nm laser irradiation group, illustrating that the mild NIR-II PTT-enhanced sonodynamic cancer therapy based on CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs afforded excellent therapeutic outcome, which could be ascribed to the mild PTT-alleviated tumor hypoxia and the high-efficiency ROS generation capacity of HJs.

Considering the enhanced SDT performance of CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs and the high tissue-penetration depth of US irradiation, we further evaluated the potential therapeutic effect of CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs for in vivo deep-tissue SDT application. As illustrated in Fig. 5g, two 4T1 tumors were established on the left and right sides of mice and the US irradiation was carried out from the left side to the right side. Fig. 5h showed that both 4T1 tumors grew rapidly in the control group. Comparatively, the mice treated with CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs under 1064 nm laser and US irradiation exhibited complete inhibition of tumor growth in the left side and right side. These results were further verified by the H&E staining of tumor sections in each group, indicating that almost all of 4T1 tumor cells were apoptosis and necrosis in the mild NIR-II PTT-enhanced SDT group (Fig. 5i). All the results illustrated that the US irradiation could penetrate through the whole body of mice to treat deep-seated tumors in the presence of CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs, which were expected to be high-efficiency sonosensitizers for deep-tissue sonodynamic cancer therapy.

We further investigated the potential adverse effect of the mild PTT and SDT based on CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs. Fig. 5c revealed that no significant decrease of the mice weight can be observed in each group, demonstrating no apparent adverse effects of CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs. The histopathological measurements, biochemical blood analysis, and hematology parameter test were then evaluated to explore the clinical translation potential of CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs. After the treatment of 18 days in each group, negligible long-term toxicity of the major organs can be verified by H&E staining (Fig. S18). Fig. S19 clearly revealed that various biochemical blood markers and normal hematology parameters in each group displayed no meaningful changes, indicating the excellent safety of CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs in vivo.

In summary, we reported, for the first time, a novel sonosensitizer based on 2D Ti3C2Tx NSs for high-efficiency SDT of deeply-seated tumors. By further assembling NIR–II–responsive CDs on the surface of Ti3C2Tx NSs, the constructed 0D/2D hybrid CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs can not only utilize the advantages of the two components but also overcome their disadvantages for enhanced SDT and PTT based on the following new effects: Firstly, the deposition of CDs improves the ROS generation efficiency of Ti3C2Tx NSs under US stimulation due to the accelerated carrier transfer and suppressive electron-hole recombination, achieving a high rate constant (0.166 min−1) for DPBF oxidation, which was much higher than that of pristine Ti3C2Tx NSs (0.070 min−1). Secondly, compared with CDs or Ti3C2Tx only, the CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs exhibited synergistically enhanced optical absorption and a high total photothermal conversion efficiency in the NIR-II window (64.5%) owing to the LSPR effect induced by enhanced carrier transfers form CDs to Ti3C2Tx, enabling realization of mild PTT at rather low laser exposure (0.6 W/cm2 for 1064 nm). Thirdly, the CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs exhibited significantly enhanced physiological stability, without showing any agglomeration in water, PBS, and DMEM culture medium. Finally, the resulting CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs were further employed for the synergistically enhanced photothermal-sonodynamic therapy, realizing complete tumor ablation due to the high-efficiency ROS generation capacity of CD@Ti3C2Tx HJs and the mild PTT-alleviated tumor hypoxia in the TME. Our work suggests the promising potential of MXene-based hybrid nanomedicine platform for cancer therapy and extensively biomedical applications.

Bijiang Geng: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Shuang Xu: Investigation, Methodology. Longxiang Shen: Investigation, Methodology. Fuling Fang: Visualization, Formal analysis. Wenyan Shi: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Dengyu Pan: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.
